howItWorks.intro: This college savings calculator uses standard formulas to provide accurate results.
rules: Future Cost = Sum of annual cost × (1+inflation)^years for each year
howItWorks.outro: Results are estimates. Consult a professional for critical decisions.

faq.question: How much does college cost?
faq.answer: In 2026, average annual costs are: public in-state ~$25,000, public out-of-state ~$45,000, private ~$60,000. These include tuition, room, board, and fees.

faq.question: Why does college inflation matter?
faq.answer: College costs have historically risen 5-8% per year — faster than general inflation. A school costing $35K today may cost $57K in 10 years at 5% inflation.

faq.question: What is a 529 plan?
faq.answer: A tax-advantaged savings account for education. Contributions grow tax-free and withdrawals for qualified education expenses are tax-free. Many states offer a state tax deduction too.

faq.question: What if my child gets scholarships?
faq.answer: Great — extra 529 funds can be used for graduate school, transferred to siblings, or rolled into a Roth IRA (up to $35K lifetime, starting 2024).

howWeCalculate.intro: This college savings calculator uses established formulas and current data to provide accurate estimates.
steps: Enter your specific values into the calculator fields above
Our algorithm applies the relevant formulas using your inputs
Results are calculated instantly in your browser — nothing is sent to a server

howWeCalculate.disclaimer: These calculations are estimates based on standard formulas. For critical decisions, always consult a qualified professional.

whenToUse.intro: This college savings calculator is designed for anyone who needs quick, reliable calculations without complex spreadsheets or professional consultations.
scenarios: When planning major financial decisions like buying a home, investing, or taking on debt
When comparing different financial products or scenarios side by side
When building a budget or projecting future savings and expenses
When you want to verify a quote or estimate from a financial advisor or lender
When teaching or learning about personal finance and compound growth
